---
title: Wet-weather setup primer
source: scaffold-sample
tags: [wet, setup, theory]
---

## Tyre pressure and warm-up

In wet sessions, drop cold tyre pressures roughly 1-2 psi below your
dry baseline. Wet tyres run cooler because the rain pulls heat out of
the carcass, so the pressure rise from cold to hot is smaller than in
dry conditions — starting lower keeps the working pressure inside the
grip window. Expect the front tyres to take an extra warm-up lap
before they bite, especially on the inters; full wets warm faster
because of the extra void.

## Damper philosophy

A softer compression setting on the front axle gives the tyre more
time on the road over standing water, which helps it find a contact
patch rather than aquaplaning across the surface. Pair it with a
slightly stiffer rebound so the front end resets quickly when you
unload it on corner exit — otherwise the car follows the tramlines
and you lose direction.

## Brake bias

Move bias forward by 1-2% in the wet. The rears unload more aggressively
under braking when grip is low, and a forward bias keeps the car from
snapping into oversteer when you trail off the pedal. If the rears
still feel light into slow corners, add another half percent — wet
brake bias is rarely "too forward."

## Driving notes drivers commonly mention

Drivers often report that the **racing line dries last** because rubber
holds water. The fastest wet line is usually off the dry line,
especially under braking. Look for the matte-grey band beside the
glossy rubber — that's where grip is highest in standing water.
